summary:
The Timurid Empire (1370–1507 AD), built by [[event:2276|Timur (Tamerlane)]] from the remnants of the [[event:2307|Chagatai Khanate]] and consolidated through three decades of brutal conquest, was one of the last great nomadic empires and arguably the most culturally brilliant — combining the military destructiveness of the Mongol tradition with a Persian cultural renaissance at the empire's Central Asian core that produced some of the most exquisite art, architecture, and scholarship of the medieval Islamic world. At its height, the empire stretched from Anatolia to the borders of India, encompassing the great Silk Road cities of Samarkand, Bukhara, and Herat.

Timur forcibly relocated the finest craftsmen, architects, poets, and scholars from every city he conquered to his capital Samarkand, transforming it into the cultural showcase of the Islamic world. The Gur-e-Amir (Timur's mausoleum), the Registan complex, and the mosques and madrasas of Samarkand represent the apogee of Timurid architectural achievement — a synthesis of Persian, Mongol, Chinese, and Byzantine decorative traditions expressed in glazed tile, soaring iwans, and monumental domes of extraordinary technical sophistication. After Timur's death (1405), his empire fragmented among his sons, but the Timurid Renaissance continued under his successors, particularly Shah Rukh and Husain Bayqarah in Herat, whose courts patronized Persian miniature painting, poetry (including the work of Jami and the early Babur, who would later found the Mughal Empire), and architecture at the highest level. The Timurid aesthetic tradition directly shaped the Mughal Empire in India and the Safavid Empire in Persia, making its legacy one of the most enduring in Islamic art history.

Timur's personal military campaigns killed an estimated 17 million people — perhaps 5% of the world's population at the time — making him one of the most lethal conquerors in history, while simultaneously creating some of its most beautiful art.
